main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Time Series Optimized Transformer for Observability (Toto)","Proportional factorized space-time attention mechanism","Student-T mixture model head","Large-scale pretraining on both proprietary observability data and open time-series datasets"],"problems":["Time series forecasting for observability metrics","Handling high-dimensional, sparse, and non-stationary time series data","Efficient and accurate forecasting for real-time system monitoring and anomaly detection","Scalability and generalization in time series models for diverse and dynamic data"],"datasets":["Proprietary observability dataset (75% of training data, large-scale, includes infrastructure and application metrics)","Open time-series datasets (including standard benchmarks such as electricity and weather forecasting)"],"metrics":["Zero-shot forecasting accuracy","Performance on standard benchmarks (e.g., electricity, weather forecasting)","Performance on observability-specific tasks"],"results":[{"metric":"Zero-shot forecasting accuracy (standard benchmarks)","value":"State-of-the-art (exact values not specified)"},{"metric":"Performance on observability-specific tasks","value":"Significantly outperforms existing models (exact values not specified)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a proportional factorized space-time attention mechanism for efficient and accurate modeling of multivariate time series.","Use of a Student-T mixture model head for robust probabilistic forecasting, generalizing Gaussian mixture models.","Training on a massive, domain-specific observability dataset, enabling superior performance on observability metrics.","Demonstration of state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on standard time series benchmarks and significant improvements on observability-specific tasks.","Scalable transformer-based architecture tailored for high-frequency, high-dimensional, and non-stationary time series data."]}

methods: Time Series Optimized Transformer for Observability (Toto)
Proportional factorized space-time attention mechanism
Student-T mixture model head
Large-scale pretraining on both proprietary observability data and open time-series datasets
problems: Time series forecasting for observability metrics
Handling high-dimensional, sparse, and non-stationary time series data
Efficient and accurate forecasting for real-time system monitoring and anomaly detection
Scalability and generalization in time series models for diverse and dynamic data
datasets: Proprietary observability dataset (75% of training data, large-scale, includes infrastructure and application metrics)
Open time-series datasets (including standard benchmarks such as electricity and weather forecasting)
metrics: Performance on standard benchmarks (e.g., electricity, weather forecasting)

results.value: Significantly outperforms existing models (exact values not specified)

novelty_claims: Introduction of a proportional factorized space-time attention mechanism for efficient and accurate modeling of multivariate time series.
Use of a Student-T mixture model head for robust probabilistic forecasting, generalizing Gaussian mixture models.
Training on a massive, domain-specific observability dataset, enabling superior performance on observability metrics.
Demonstration of state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on standard time series benchmarks and significant improvements on observability-specific tasks.
Scalable transformer-based architecture tailored for high-frequency, high-dimensional, and non-stationary time series data.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["AutoMixer: a time-series foundation model approach combining AutoEncoder-based channel-compressed pretraining with TSMixer for multivariate time-series forecasting","Channel-compressed pretrain and finetune workflow: pretraining an AutoEncoder to compress channels, then integrating with TSMixer and finetuning end-to-end for downstream tasks"],"problems":["Accurate multivariate forecasting of business KPIs (Biz-KPIs) using BizITObs data, which fuses business and IT event channels as multivariate time series","Decoupling useful and noisy inter-channel interactions in BizITObs data to improve forecasting accuracy","Enhancing cross-channel correlation modeling for better Biz-KPI forecasts"],"datasets":["4 new BizITObs datasets (publicly released, specific names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Mean Squared Error (MSE)","Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC)"],"results":[{"metric":"MSE reduction","value":"11-15% improvement over state-of-the-art models"},{"metric":"PCC enhancement","value":"1-2X improvement over state-of-the-art models"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of AutoMixer, a time-series foundation model approach specifically for BizITObs data, using a novel channel-compressed pretrain and finetune workflow","Novel use of AutoEncoders to compress input channels (not just hidden features), reducing noisy channel interactions and amplifying TSMixer's effectiveness","Demonstration of the ease of porting channel-compressed pretrained models across several BizITObs downstream tasks, leveraging foundation model workflows","Public release of 4 new BizITObs datasets and comprehensive benchmarking against state-of-the-art forecasting models"]}

methods: AutoMixer: a time-series foundation model approach combining AutoEncoder-based channel-compressed pretraining with TSMixer for multivariate time-series forecasting
Channel-compressed pretrain and finetune workflow: pretraining an AutoEncoder to compress channels, then integrating with TSMixer and finetuning end-to-end for downstream tasks
problems: Accurate multivariate forecasting of business KPIs (Biz-KPIs) using BizITObs data, which fuses business and IT event channels as multivariate time series
Decoupling useful and noisy inter-channel interactions in BizITObs data to improve forecasting accuracy
Enhancing cross-channel correlation modeling for better Biz-KPI forecasts
datasets: 4 new BizITObs datasets (publicly released, specific names not provided in the excerpt)
novelty_claims: Introduction of AutoMixer, a time-series foundation model approach specifically for BizITObs data, using a novel channel-compressed pretrain and finetune workflow
Novel use of AutoEncoders to compress input channels (not just hidden features), reducing noisy channel interactions and amplifying TSMixer's effectiveness
Demonstration of the ease of porting channel-compressed pretrained models across several BizITObs downstream tasks, leveraging foundation model workflows
Public release of 4 new BizITObs datasets and comprehensive benchmarking against state-of-the-art forecasting models

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Chronos framework: tokenizes time series values using scaling and quantization into a fixed vocabulary, then trains transformer-based language models (T5 family) on forecasting accuracy","value":"Chronos significantly outperforms other methods on datasets that were part of the training corpus"},{"metric":"Zero-shot forecasting accuracy","value":"Chronos has outperforming or matching specialized models.","Integration of data augmentation techniques (TSMixup and KernelSynth) to address the scarcity and diversity limitations of time series methods: Chronos framework: tokenizes time series values using scaling and quantization into a fixed vocabulary, then trains transformer-based language models (T5 family) on these tokenized sequences via cross-entropy loss

Data augmentation strategies: TSMixup (convex combination of base time series from different datasets) and KernelSynth (synthetic time series generation using Gaussian processes with random kernel composition)
Pretraining on a large collection of public and synthetic time series datasets
problems: General-purpose time series forecasting across diverse domains
Zero-shot forecasting: achieving strong performance on unseen datasets without task-specific training
Overcoming the scarcity and limited diversity of public time series datasets for model training
Reducing the need for prompt engineering, fine-tuning, or large-scale models for time series tasks
datasets: Large collection of publicly available time series datasets (42 datasets in total, specific names not provided in the excerpt)
Synthetic dataset generated via Gaussian processes (KernelSynth)
metrics: Forecasting accuracy (not specified, but likely includes standard time series forecasting metrics such as MAPE, RMSE, etc.)
Zero-shot forecasting performance (relative accuracy on unseen datasets)

results.value: Chronos significantly outperforms other methods on datasets that were part of the training corpus
Chronos has comparable and occasionally superior performance on new datasets relative to methods trained specifically on them

novelty_claims: Chronos is a simple, minimally adapted language modeling framework for time series forecasting that uses standard transformer architectures without time-series-specific modifications.
Chronos tokenizes real-valued time series into discrete tokens, enabling the use of off-the-shelf language models for time series forecasting.
Chronos achieves strong zero-shot forecasting performance without task-specific adjustments or large model sizes, outperforming or matching specialized models.
Integration of data augmentation techniques (TSMixup and KernelSynth) to address the scarcity and diversity limitations of time series datasets.
Chronos demonstrates that pretrained language models can serve as general-purpose, foundation models for time series forecasting.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["PatchTST: Channel-independent patch time series Transformer","Segmentation of time series into subseries-level patches as input tokens","Channel-independence: each channel (univariate time series) shares embedding and Transformer weights","Self-supervised pre-training and fine-tuning for time series forecasting"],"problems":["Efficient and accurate long-term multivariate time series forecasting with Transformers","Reducing computation and memory usage in Transformer-based time series models","Improving representation learning for time series data","Transferring learned representations across datasets"],"datasets":["Traffic","Electricity","Weather"],"metrics":["Mean Squared Error (MSE)","Running time (seconds)"],"results":[{"metric":"MSE (PatchTST, L=336, patch, self-supervised)","value":"0.349"},{"metric":"MSE (PatchTST, L=336, no patch)","value":"0.397"},{"metric":"MSE (Channel-independent, L=380, down-sampled)","value":"0.447"},{"metric":"MSE (Channel-independent, L=96)","value":"0.518"},{"metric":"MSE (DLinear, L=336)","value":"0.410"},{"metric":"MSE (FEDFormer, L=336)","value":"0.597"},{"metric":"Running time (Traffic, w. patch)","value":"464s"},{"metric":"Running time (Traffic, w.o. patch)","value":"10040s"},{"metric":"Running time (Electricity, w. methods: PatchTST: Channel-independent patch time series Transformer
Segmentation of time series into subseries-level patches as input tokens
Channel-independence: each channel (univariate time series) shares embedding and Transformer weights
Self-supervised pre-training and fine-tuning for time series forecasting
problems: Efficient and accurate long-term multivariate time series forecasting with Transformers
Reducing computation and memory usage in Transformer-based time series models
Improving representation learning for time series data
Transferring learned representations across datasets
novelty_claims: First application of channel-independence to Transformer-based models for time series forecasting
Introduction of patching (subseries-level patches) as input tokens for Transformers in time series, retaining local semantic information and reducing computation/memory quadratically
Demonstration that patching enables longer look-back windows without increasing computational cost, improving forecasting accuracy
PatchTST achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results in both supervised and self-supervised settings, outperforming previous Transformer-based and linear models
Self-supervised pre-training with PatchTST outperforms supervised training on large datasets and enables effective transfer learning across datasets

novelty_claims: First work to systematically challenge the effectiveness of Transformer-based models for long-term time series forecasting (LTSF)

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Frequency Enhanced Decomposed Transformer (FEDformer)","Seasonal-trend decomposition integrated with Transformer","Fourier enhanced blocks in Transformer","Wavelet enhanced blocks in Transformer","Random selection of Fourier components for sparse representation"],"problems":["Long-term time series forecasting","High computational complexity of Transformer-based models for long sequences","Inability of Transformers to capture global properties (overall trend/distribution) of time series","Maintaining global statistics and distribution in time series predictions"],"datasets":["ETTm1","Six benchmark datasets across energy, weather, traffic, economics, and disease domains (exact names not all specified in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Prediction error (likely MSE or MAE, though not explicitly stated)","Kolmogorov-Smirnov distribution test"],"results":[{"metric":"Prediction error reduction (multivariate)","value":"14.8%"},{"metric":"Prediction error reduction (univariate)","value":"22.6%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a frequency enhanced decomposed Transformer (FEDformer) architecture with mixture of experts for seasonal-trend decomposition to better capture global properties of time series.","Introduces Fourier enhanced blocks and Wavelet enhanced blocks as replacements for self-attention and cross-attention in Transformer, enabling frequency domain mapping.","Achieves linear computational complexity and memory cost by randomly selecting a fixed number of Fourier components, verified both theoretically and empirically.","Demonstrates significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods on six benchmark datasets for both multivariate and univariate forecasting."]}

methods: Frequency Enhanced Decomposed Transformer (FEDformer)
Seasonal-trend decomposition integrated with Transformer
Fourier enhanced blocks in Transformer
Wavelet enhanced blocks in Transformer
Random selection of Fourier components for sparse representation
problems: Long-term time series forecasting
High computational complexity of Transformer-based models for long sequences
Inability of Transformers to capture global properties (overall trend/distribution) of time series
Maintaining global statistics and distribution in time series predictions
datasets: Six benchmark datasets across energy, weather, traffic, economics, and disease domains (exact names not all specified in the provided text)
metrics: Prediction error (likely MSE or MAE, though not explicitly stated)
novelty_claims: Proposes a frequency enhanced decomposed Transformer (FEDformer) architecture with mixture of experts for seasonal-trend decomposition to better capture global properties of time series.
Introduces Fourier enhanced blocks and Wavelet enhanced blocks as replacements for self-attention and cross-attention in Transformer, enabling frequency domain mapping.
Achieves linear computational complexity and memory cost by randomly selecting a fixed number of Fourier components, verified both theoretically and empirically.
Demonstrates significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods on six benchmark datasets for both multivariate and univariate forecasting.

novelty_claims: Proposes a factorized-attention-based model (CaFA) specifically designed for spherical geometries in global weather forecasting

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Autoformer: a decomposition-based Transformer architecture for time series forecasting","Progressive decomposition blocks as inner operators in the model","Auto-Correlation mechanism for dependencies discovery and information aggregation at the series (sub-series) level, replacing self-attention"],"problems":["Long-term time series forecasting","Modeling intricate temporal patterns in long-term future predictions","Overcoming computational inefficiency and information bottleneck in Transformer-based models for long sequences"],"datasets":["Six benchmarks covering five applications: energy, traffic, economics, weather, and disease (exact dataset names not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Forecasting accuracy (relative improvement)"],"results":[{"metric":"Relative improvement in forecasting accuracy","value":"38% (on six benchmarks)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Autoformer, a novel decomposition architecture for time series forecasting, integrating decomposition as an inner block rather than a pre-processing step.","Introduces the Auto-Correlation mechanism, which discovers dependencies and aggregates information at the sub-series level based on series periodicity, replacing self-attention.","Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and significant efficiency improvements (O(L log L) complexity) for long-term forecasting tasks."]}

novelty_claims: Proposes Autoformer, a novel decomposition architecture for time series forecasting, integrating decomposition as an inner block rather than a pre-processing step.

Leverages the property that temporal relations are preserved after downsampling, enabling effective multi-resolution temporal feature extraction.
Demonstrates that SCINet achieves superior forecasting accuracy compared to both convolutional and Transformer-based models.

methods: ViGAT: a pure-attention bottom-up approach for video event recognition and explanation
Object detection using Faster R-CNN to extract objects from frames
Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone for feature extraction from objects and frames
ViGAT head: a network of three Graph Attention Network (GAT) blocks factorized along spatial and temporal dimensions
Weight-tying of GAT blocks to reduce memory footprint
WiD (Weighted in-Degree) based explanation criteria for identifying salient objects and frames
Quantitative evaluation of explanations using adapted XAI measures (IC, AD, F-, F+)
problems: Automatic recognition of high-level events and actions in unconstrained videos
Providing explainable AI (XAI) explanations for video event recognition decisions, both at object and frame level
Modeling both local (object-level) and long-term (temporal) dependencies in video for event recognition
Reducing computational and memory complexity in bottom-up video event recognition models
novelty_claims: First pure-attention bottom-up approach for video event recognition, combining object detection, ViT backbone, and a fully-attention GAT-based head factorized along spatial and temporal dimensions.
ViGAT enables extraction of both object-level (spatial) and frame-level (temporal) explanations for event recognition decisions, using WiD scores from learned adjacency matrices.
First to quantitatively evaluate the quality of temporal (frame) explanations in video event recognition using adapted XAI measures from image recognition.
Demonstrates state-of-the-art event recognition performance on three large-scale video datasets, outperforming previous top-down and bottom-up methods.
Employs weight-tying across GAT blocks to reduce memory footprint without sacrificing performance.
Provides a comprehensive ablation study showing the importance of bottom-up object features, the complementarity of object and frame features, and the effect of weight-tying and GAT depth.
